turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: We'll hear arguments next in No. 362, McGee against the Uni~ed States. Mr. Levine. ARGUMENT OF ALAN H. LEVINE, ESQ.

turns.name: LEVINE
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court. This case is before you on Writ of Certiorari to the Second Circuit. Petitioner seeks review of convictions on 4 counts, under the I1ilitary Selective Sefvice Act, 1967. Tho cbief question presented by this petition .l:s the question left open in McKart v, the United States. It presents the pure question of whether, where a registrant presents a claim for consciencious objection, and that claim is denied, without a basis in fact, as the Second Circuit con- ceeds here, it was so denied, where therefore the classieication the I-A classification, and the induction order, were illegal, is he barred from challenging that classification and induction order in judicial review? In answering that question "no", \te wi:Cl assert two basic propositions. One, that the exhaustion of administrative remedies doctrine is inappropriately applied, where, as McKart suggested, its effect is not merely to delay jud::.cial review, ~.nd we will defend secondly, the proposition that if exhaustion of administrative ren.edies is to be applied so harshly, it must be applied with Congr£.ssional mandate. And that the Militax:y Selective Service Act of :_967 to the contrary does not limit judicial -:-eview, in defr.nse to a criminal pro·- secution, but in fact guarantees judic.i al review in defense ·co a criminal prosecution. Petitioner at ·che time of induction was a full time his local board at that time that he was preparing for the In August, 1964, he enrolled in the University of Rochester and the next month was given a II-S del'errment, fo:,:- all practical purposes kept that deferi:ment m11:il September 1167. In February 1966, while still at the University of Rochester and still possessing a II-S classification, he appliad for conscien cous objec:tor status, pursuant to regulations, th~ local board deferred consideratf_on of that application until h.;i was no longer entitled to a lower clasEification. In March of 1966, he got a letter from the local board to that effect, that they would cefer consideration of his c.o. classificatS.on. In April of 1967, he was still at Rochester, still possessing a II-s, Petitioner wrote tile President of the United States. He enclosed in that letter remnanta of a tern and burnt draft card. in conneciton with that? A He ,,,as not, nor was he indicted in the indictments under review here. Subsequent to the trial in ·.:his case, he was indicted on 2 counts of destroying a draft card. Those cases have not come to trial, Your Honor. Q That question isn't forced, at all? A It is not, Your Honor. He wrote the President in April, 1969, and he ad- vised the President that he would probably be em:itled to a theologiaal deferrrnent, nevertheless, he said, "I mus 1: sever every link with violence a.nd war.• He expressed, again quoting, and not hate." As a result he said that he could no longer cooperate with the Selective Service System. In June of 1967·, upon his graduation f:rom Rochester, he was sent a currer.~ information questionnaire. SSS Form 127. He returned that questionnaire unopened and said that he would return all future correspondence unopened. In September, 1967, he was classified I-A, despite his pending C.O. application. He returned that I-A classificatic card, Q Now that c,o, application that you just characterized as pending, is one that was filed Hhile he was a student at the University of Rochester? A That's right, In March of 1966--- Q 3 years earlier, 3~years earliert A No, this was in September 1967 that I:m talking about. Q A year and a half, approximately, A Right, Q And at that time the board had indicatad that since he had a II-S student deferrment, thera was no need for them to consider the C.O. application? A In March of 1966, The board chairnan testified at trial somewhat ambiguously, and as a result of his testimony, as to what the board did in March 66, and Sep- tember, 67, that led Judge Feinberg to conclude that in fact the board had never considered the application on its merits, chairmans testimony, they had just reaffirmed their earlier decision, Q Why did they send him the information form? A Presumably because they were aware from his would have been a place to talk about his current status, I suppose. A It was a current informs r:1.on form. Q As to what status he might be ::laiming, or--- A !:l'~lJ., the board had before it his claim as to conscientious objection. Q Well, tilat waa a year and a half ago, yes. A It was a yea-: and a couple of ll'.onths be- fore it, that's correat, Your Honor. Q If he had an.3wered it, h"' would ha•re then A If he had told the board at that time that does not contain feference to conscientious objection. cation. As I said, Judge Feinberg thinks that they did not consider his application on the merits atall, the trial juage, however, found that as a matter of fact, at the close of the trial that the application had been considered. Q And rejected. A Pa:cdon? Q And reje.cted. A And rejected. That's cc.,;,::rect. that month, he enrolled in Unicn Theological Seminary, In October of 1967 he was ordered to--- Q Did he recieve his I-A classification, i.e. A He did sent it back unopened, Your Honor. Q So he didn't even know that h,? 'd been classified I-A, is that xight? I-A undpened, later that month enrolled in Union Theological Seminai:y. In October of 1967 he ~as sen~ an otder to report fox physical examination. He returned that orde:c unopc?I'.ed. He was ordered to report for induction in Jan~ary of 1969. He filled out a number of forms at that ti 1e giving a 1/'a.. iety of different j kinds cf information at that time. He took a corr.pleb? physical Q So he did open that envelope. A Pardon? Q He did open that envelope? A He did open that envelope. He reported as Q Now, that was the physical examination A It's given by the military for the Belec- q At the time of induction, , or quite a A No, this was at the time of induction. His file was then forwarded to the United States ttorney, significantly, with a :recommendation by the Select:.v,? uction and failure to possess his notice of class~fication. Q Is there any indication why he changed his ind regarding how he would deal with mail addressed to him by A ~y he opened the--- Q He sent several of them back, including his A I--- Q And then when there arrived other com- munication, one directing him to report for physical examinatior he not only opened the communication but complied wit.1 it. Is there any indication of why? A Not from the record Your Honor. I did not represent Petitioner at the time, perhaps he spo~e to legal counsel at that time, I donnot know and the record does not indicate. Despite the recommendation of Selertive service, that he be prosecuted on 2 counts, in February of 1968 the government sought and obtained a 4 count indictmant . The second was for refusal to take the physical examinatior. The third was for failure to possess the 11otice of The fourth, for failure to dubmit the informacion requested on the classification for111. Q Incidentally, these notices af classificatic- and to report for a physical, are they in sealed envelopes or are they post cards? Your Honor . all 4 counts, sentenced to 2 year concurrent sentences on each count. In March of 1970, the Second Circuit upheld the convictions, Judge Feinberg dissented. The three judges in the Second Circuit conceeded that there was no basis in fact for the board to deny Petitionex· conscientious objector claim. Nevertheless, they said that because he failed to exhaust his appea .. remedies wil:hin Selec- tive Service, they would declide to review the classification. Service Act provides for review of classificaitons, "as a defense to a criminal prosecution". It does not qualigy that right, it does not conditioniit, on exhausing adminis~rative remedies. It says rather that i"i:; can be raised, ;ceviet1 can be induction. It's important to note, Your Honors, that the Congress not an inadvertance that ~his aspect of judicial review is stated as it is. They had it before it, they considered the question of judicial review and limited only pre··induction revie,-;. The question before this Court, ..i.n Oesteroich. As the Senate Armed Services Committed said, "lf the registrant does not submit to induction, he may raise as a defense to a criminal prosecution the issue of the legality of the cl~ssification. Q The Court of Appeals theory, concession as is bas?d on the conscientious objector claim rather than en i;h,3 attendance at a theological school, as I w1dersta11d it. A That i.s correct. Tl",ey deal wi·~,1 that the is'3ue upon which Judge Feinberg relied in dissent was the Q Whnt, there is some qum,:tion, I suppose, Seminary is sufficient, is not sufficient ur.der the law, not mere attendance at a theological seminary, is it? A Well, let me say briefly, Yow: Honor, th .. t six G is--- Q Divinity school deferments? ·---- That's right. Is mandatory. He hhall be eferred assuming hemeets certain qualifications, ·hat's full ime attendance, under the direction of a recogni::ed church. Q And studying to become a--- A That is correct. Now, McGee presented, not directly to his board, but it car, be inferred from the record that the board had notice of the, fact that he was a·i:tending Union Theologi,,al Seminary. He die not request the deferrment, some cases ha•,e suggested that it's not necessary. We point out ·the difference of the language of 6-G and the language providing for student defer- rrnents which requires an affirrnent of request by the registrant. The language fof 6-G is "shall be deferred". Q Not for mere attenda118e, though. I thoug':l·i: you and I had agreed. A Under--- Q We can --- a man who is just likes to dabble around philosoph:i.cally and has plenty of l:ime and money so he goes to various theologicaJ: schools ~n order to intent ever of becoming a minister.of the gospel, of a pasC:oral sophical and religious knowledge. That man wouldn't be subject to statutory deferr- mean so far as the record shows we don't know whether this was his purpose. A Well, the trial court found ·;hat he was in full time attendance and that he was pursuing a degree lead- What the trial court did not find is that he was there under the direction of a recognized church since Union church, which is non sectariaG. There is no evidence according to the trial judge, in the record, that he was there under the direction of a re- cognized church. A That is a statutory requirement. Q And that's the neceesary~-- brief that Selective Service Procedures under such circumstances authorize a board to look into the basis for a deferrment and liminary information. The major point to which I would like to address my- Professor Davis tells ,,25 it i.s a doctrine concerned And even when used in affirmative litiga·tion there Q You take the same position, I take it, if oard? A No, we would not. Q Why not? Q Then why not? A Because I belie•re the act requires minimally rimary jurisdiction of the local board. We urge a distinction, s does Davis in his treaty between primary jurisdiction and khaus~ion. You are entitled to review of your clasuification, il: our classification is not supported by any facts, then a court could not properly go into anything that was not befoke the local board. McGee here, by concession of the Second Circuit did present to tbe local board what was necessary to support his classification. We don't quarrel with the fact that ht? has the burden of prooving his classification. We only say tha,; he met that burden. Q But you do say that he had to proove to his draft board, too? A That's correct. And we suggest here that Q But he didn 't have to proove it to sorle A That•a right. That he is entitled in Court to judicial review of his classification, his classification without a basis in fact, The effect of the application of the exhaustion rule in this case, and in criminal cases generally, is to work a very substantial denial of a right in a criminal prosecution. The difference between the applicaiton of that rule in an affirroent of a defensive case was elaborated on by Chief Judge Magruder in two cases cited in our brief, the Smith and lllcCril- lis cases, and there the judge said that we do not have to a apply the e>:haustion rule in the defensive context, it is a namely Yakus and Myers vs. Bethlehem Shipbuilding. And the Court there said those were specifically That brings us to McKart. !-lcKart, we subnit, reflectn istiactions between affirmative and defensive context. It pointi, ut that in the defensive context it is an exceedingly harsh The McKart court suggests that maybe there is a dif- erence between the legal issue presented there ar.cl the CO clal m he co claim, we submit, is a legal issue, you take, as the ourt does, any basis in fact case before it, an indisputed s~i. issue which confromts this Court whether or not there is exhau;- tion. There is no different burdan. The question then, is are other registrants going to do what 11cGee did? Rec0911izing, as we must, that what McGee did, he did for, out of reasons of moral scruples. But will other registrants do it for tactical rea- think deserves special attention. The government says, "If we here, decline to appeal his administrative remedies, then the well counseled registrant is going to submit a bare Form 150, ill provide on its face no basis for denial, it will rtot 150 .. __ _ Q And the Form 150 is the Conscientious Ob·· jectors form? A It is, Your Honor. At that point he will right to a personal appearance, not that he must personally ppear. ltcGee was not told he must personally appear, only tha,: e had a right to appear. Refuse to appear, told that he has right to appeal, and refuse to appeal. lie will then refuse nduction and have perfect defense before the Court. There will e no basis in fact upon which a Court could uphold the I-A classification. just make ou~ a primae facaie case before hislibocal board--- Q Well don't y~u think in your hypothetica:, case there would be plenty of basis in fact for the support of a I-A classification if the man had not supmittad anything to support the conscientious objector--- A Well the governments thesis :,s that he it was not a bona fide claim. submission as possible. And--- erity, the remedy is simple, we attach in our reply brief, New there's a space there for the local board to check off, send it to the registrant, s.ay, you are ordered to appeai~ before the local board to discuss your claim. 'l'hat is the simple remedy available to the board in cases where tee~• find themselves in doubt. If they are not in doubt, their obligaiton is to classify I-0. ~ney can't simply have vague doubts, Dickenson and --- teach us tha·c thcra must be affirmative evidence. No* it might be affirmative evidence that he fails doubts, as I suggest, he can be called in. Q Aie you suggesting that the b:>ard has an all the mat.erial that he wishes to submt!:.? A No. We suggest that under currant Selectiva Service procedures, the board has put itself, and the sy.stem has appearanca. The sequence :'.s tha~ you st.bmit your claim, you ara classified, then you have your right to personal appearance. Now if that'a the way the se:ective Service system fication. If it is an inadequate claim, then either you have not met your burden of prooving your entitlement to the claim, and the board then is obliged to state its reasons why H:, you have not met your claim. Or if the board then is left in doubt, then since the registrant has made out his primae facaie case, it is up to the board then to find some affirmative evidence and if it needs a personal appearance, then it ce1n order that personal appearance. Q I'm at a loss, ;.1r. Levine, to understand registrant could play games with the board. A That's the governments point. Q Yes. Well you were merely re!:erring to the gcvennments point.
